One of my favorite things to collect in Japan!

Goshuin!! (御朱印) ⛩️🤍

Goshuin are sacred seals you can receive at shrines and temples as a record of your visit and prayer

‼️They’re not meant to be “tourist stamps”, they’re a spiritual and cultural tradition connected to pilgrimage and worship in Japan!

You collect them in a special notebook called a goshuinchō (御朱印帳), which you can buy at many shrines, temples, stationery stores, and even places like Loft or Tokyu Hands🔖

Every goshuin is unique and usually includes beautiful calligraphy, stamps, the shrine or temple’s name, and the date of your visit. Some are seasonal or only available during festivals, which makes them even more special!🎌

Most goshuin cost around ¥300–¥1000, and the fee helps support the care and preservation of the shrine or temple💴

Some places will write directly into your goshuinchō, while others may give you a separate sheet to paste in later📖

A few respectful things to know:

⛩️ Visit the shrine or temple properly before requesting one
🤫 Be quiet and mindful while waiting
🚫 Don’t use a goshuinchō for regular souvenir stamps
🛐 Treat your goshuinchō respectfully since many people consider it sacred

If you’re nervous about asking, you can politely say:
“Goshuin onegaishimasu” (御朱印お願いします / ごしゅいん おねがいします)
which means “May I please have a goshuin?”

You can also ask:
“Goshuin wa doko desu ka?” (御朱印はどこですか？ / ごしゅいん は どこ です か)
which means “Where can I receive a goshuin?”

Honestly, collecting goshuin completely changed the way I traveled around Japan. My goshuinchō feels less like a souvenir and more like a personal record of all the meaningful places that I visited on my journey🤍

Which Day Trip would you do?

The biggest mistake people make when planning a Tokyo itinerary is treating Tokyo like one city. It isn’t. 

The smartest Japan travel strategy is planning by area, not by attraction. If you randomly jump from Asakusa to Shibuya to Odaiba in one day, you’ll spend half your Tokyo trip inside subway tunnels instead of actually enjoying Japan.

So here’s the framework that made my Tokyo itinerary feel smooth instead of exhausting.

Day one is old Tokyo meets electric Tokyo. Start in Asakusa with Sensoji Temple, Nakamise Street snacks, kimono rentals, and Tokyo’s traditional atmosphere. Then move toward Akihabara where the city suddenly turns into anime stores, claw machines, retro arcades, manga shops. 

Day two is fashion, street culture, and modern Tokyo energy. Harajuku gives you Takeshita Street, vintage shopping, crepes, and Meiji Jingu Shrine hidden inside a massive forest in the middle of the city. Then Shibuya takes over at night with Shibuya Crossing, Shibuya Sky sunset views, neon streets, izakayas, shopping, and some of the best nightlife energy in Japan.

Day three belongs entirely to Shinjuku. Trust me, this district alone can consume a full day. Start peaceful with Shinjuku Gyoen National Garden, head up the free Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building observation deck for skyline views, then let the chaos begin at night with, Omoide Yokocho, Godzilla Road, ramen shops, and late night Tokyo wandering.

Day four shifts east toward Tokyo Bay. Ginza for luxury shopping and omakase sushi experiences. Toyosu Fish Market for fresh seafood and tuna bowls. Then Odaiba for futuristic Tokyo. TeamLab Planets Tokyo, Rainbow Bridge views, giant Gundam statue, waterfront sunsets, and one of the prettiest nighttime skyline views in the city.

Day five is your day trip day, Kamakura gives you the Great Buddha and coastal temple vibes. Hakone gives you onsens and Mount Fuji views. Nikko gives you UNESCO shrines and forest mountains. Yokohama gives you a quieter port city feel with Chinatown and waterfront evening

Leaving the busy center of Tokyo and arriving in a quiet residential neighborhood, the journey to this cat village begins by hopping on a cute cat-themed tram.

The area around Gotokuji, often called Tokyo’s Cat Village, is known for a legend from the Edo period in which a cat saved a feudal lord. Since then, cats have come to symbolize good luck here.

From a temple filled with thousands of cat statues to cute cat sculptures, maneki-neko shaped desserts, and souvenirs, there are cats to discover throughout the neighborhood.

Escaping the crowds and unlocking Japan’s hidden gems 🚐💨

⛩️ Takaya Shrine (Torii in the Sky)
Instead of driving, Amber and I opted for the hiking route from the bottom. The breathtaking view of the Seto Inland Sea through the torii gate is absolutely worth the sweat!

🥾 The Hike:
 Ascent: 40–50 min (Intense cardio on steep steps!)
 Descent: 30–40 min
 Total distance: ~1.5km. Wear proper shoes.

🅿️ Parking & Access (Crucial Info):
 Hikers: Free parking is available at the Lower Shrine (Shimomiya).
 Drivers: The mountain road is extremely narrow. On weekends/holidays, private cars are BANNED from driving to the summit (a shuttle bus from the base is required).

Seeing Mount Fuji isn’t just about luck — it’s about timing it right.
Many people don’t realize that Mount Fuji is visible only around 80 days a year. If you randomly visit the Fuji area, there’s a good chance you might not see it at all.

Before my trip, I checked https://fuji-san.info/en/index.html, which shows the 7-day Mount Fuji visibility forecast. It really helped me pick the right day.

But here’s something important: even if the forecast shows 10/10 visibility, it doesn’t mean Fuji will be clear the entire day. For me it was 10/10, but it was visible only in the morning and again around sunset.
My advice: arrive a day earlier and stay overnight so you can catch both views.
If you want to explore multiple Mount Fuji viewpoints, keep at least two days in your itinerary. One day for the north side around Kawaguchiko, and another for the south side around Shizuoka. The viewpoints are quite far from each other, and checking the live cameras ( https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/fujisanwatcher/live/) during the day can help you decide where to go.
Plan your trip a little smartly, keep your itinerary flexible, and you can definitely see Mount Fuji.

Have you heard of goshuin in Japan? ⛩️

Goshuin are handwritten seals you receive at shrines and temples to mark your visit! They are not regular tourist stamps‼️

They’re collected in a special notebook called a goshuinchō (御朱印帳), sold at shrines, temples, and stationery stores 🔖

Each goshuin is unique and usually includes calligraphy, stamps, the shrine or temple name, and the date. Some are only available during certain seasons or festivals, making them unique to collect! 🎌

Most goshuin cost around ¥300 to ¥1000 (with special edition ones around ¥1000 to ¥3000), and the fee helps support the shrine or temple 💴

A few things to know:

⛩️ Visit the shrine or temple before requesting one
🤫 Be quiet and respectful while waiting
🚫 Don’t use a goshuinchō for regular souvenir stamps
🛐 Many people consider goshuinchō sacred, so treat it respectfully

To ask for one, you can say “Goshuin onegaishimasu” (御朱印お願いします)

Collecting goshuin changed the way I traveled through Japan. My goshuinchō feels like a collection of meaningful memories from every place I visited 🤍

In the rural valleys of northern Kyoto lies Ohara, a peaceful village famous for historic temples and farming traditions. 🥰

Not so far from Jakko-in temple there’s the area’s only foot bath cafe. ♨️
At Ohara Sansou Foot Bath Cafe, you can soothe your tired feet in genuine, warm mineral hot spring water while looking out over a quiet Japanese garden. 😍

It operates on a simple one-drink minimum system. Prices start around ¥900, which includes your choice of drink, up to 40 minutes in the foot bath, and a small towel to dry your feet (so you don’t need to bring anything with you!).

☕️ Try their Homemade Shiso Juice! Ohara is famous throughout Japan for cultivating premium red perilla, and this drink is incredibly refreshing after a morning of walking.

🚌 From JR Kyoto Station, take Kyoto Bus No. 17 directly to the Ohara terminal (approx. 60 mins). 

⚠️ Important Note: the foot bath cafe is currently only open on Saturdays, Sundays, and National Holidays from 11:00 AM to 5:00 PM. Keep this in mind when planning your trek!

He can’t speak but still runs his shop solo🥺
One of the sweetest encounters I had in Tokyo was at this jazz izakaya run by the sweetest 85 year old chef, where he has lost his voice due to throat cancer but is still deeply passionate about cooking. I ordered the dishes he recommended so he could really showcase his skills, and every item was made with so much care. The standouts for me were his tamagoyaki, chicken meatballs, and curry udon! We communicated through a translator and he used his iPad to respond in English, which made the whole experience even more special. The restaurant is entirely run by him, so please be patient when you visit! He told me he trained in Kyoto and that the shop has been around since 1948, originally started by his father. Save this spot if you’re looking for incredible food and a cozy jazz atmosphere in Tokyo, and send it to someone who loves discovering local hidden gems over trendy spots! 
📍 Matsushima
Address: Japan, 〒170-0005 Tokyo, Toshima City, Minamiotsuka, 1 Chome−1−51-18 高橋ビル

This is Nachi Falls, Japan.

The train ride is closer to 4 hours from Osaka, but it goes by faster than you'd think. You follow the coastline all the way to this small fishing town called Kii-Katsura. From there, it's a short taxi or bus ride to this waterfall. 

Give yourself a full day or even stay overnight to really enjoy it.

They say this place was sacred long before religion had a name. At 436 ft (133m), Nachi Falls is the tallest single-drop waterfall in Japan. Monks once trained here under freezing water for spiritual discipline.

Standing here., you feel why they consider this place sacred. #japan

Perfect 15-Day Japan Itinerary 🇯🇵

Save this for your Japan trip planning 🇯🇵
Follow for more travel tips✨️

Day 1 — Tokyo (Shibuya & Shinjuku)
Shibuya Crossing, Hachiko Statue, Shibuya Sky, Golden Gai & Omoide Yokocho nightlife.

Day 2 — Hakone
Owakudani Sulphur Valley, Hakone Ropeway & Lake Ashi Pirate Cruise with Mt Fuji views.

Day 3–4 — Kawaguchiko (Mt Fuji Area)
Lake Kawaguchiko cycling, Fuji Panoramic Ropeway, Oishi Park, Momiji Corridor & the iconic Chureito Pagoda views.

Day 5–6 — Osaka
Osaka Castle, Osaka Aquarium Kaiyukan, Dotonbori food streets, Katsuo-ji Temple & Minoh Falls.

Day 7–10 — Kyoto Region
Nara Deer Park & Todai-ji, Arashiyama Bamboo Grove, Togetsukyo Bridge, Kiyomizu-dera, Yasaka Pagoda, Philosopher’s Path, Fushimi Inari Shrine, Kurama–Kibune hike & TeamLab Kyoto.

Day 11–15 — Tokyo
Sensoji Temple, Sumida River walk, Tokyo Skytree, Tokyo Tower, Roppongi Hills, Imperial Palace, Meiji Shrine, Shinjuku Gyoen gardens, Akihabara & Harry Potter Studio.

📖 유후인 여행기

이곳을 꼭 가고 싶다고 노래노래를 부르고 이렇게 찍겠다고 영상을 몇번씩 돌려보며 결연하게 다짐하는 나를 보고 친구가 신기해하며 물었다.

“너는 그런 걸 찍는 게 좋은거야? 아니면 인스타에 올리기 위해서 방문하는 거야?”

거기에 나는 이렇게 답했었다.

Tokyo’s classic pudding café 🍮

Located near Toranomon Station, Hekkelun ヘッケルン is famous for its giant custard pudding — soft, rich, and topped with slightly bitter caramel.

The pudding is made by an 80+ year-old master who has been perfecting this one dessert for over 50 years. His dedication to a single craft truly shows the spirit of Japanese craftsmanship.

The café is small with about 20 seats, and the pudding often sells out before 12PM so it’s best to come early.

⏰ 9:00–15:00
❌ Closed Wed, Sat, Sun
🍮 Jumbo Pudding & Coffee Set ~¥900

東京經典昭和風布丁店 🍮

位於 Toranomon Station 附近的 Hekkelun ヘッケルン，以 巨大手工焦糖布丁 聞名。口感綿密滑順，搭配微苦焦糖，簡單卻讓人一吃就喜歡。

店裡製作布丁的爺爺已經 80多歲，專注做這一道甜點 超過50年。能夠一輩子把一件事做到極致，真的很有日本職人的精神。

店內只有約 20個座位，布丁常常 12點前就賣完，想吃建議早點來。

⏰ 9:00–15:00
❌ 週三、週六、週日公休

Is it a concert ticket or an observation deck 😭

If Shibuya Sky is on your Tokyo itinerary, treat it like a concert ticket, not an observation deck.

- Book exactly 14 days before your visit at midnight Japan time. Sunset slots during peak Japan travel seasons can disappear in under 2 minutes.

- Have the official Shibuya Sky site, Klook, and KKday open at the same time. Many international travelers find their cards rejected on the official website, while Klook and KKday often have separate inventory.

- The biggest mistake? Booking the actual sunset time. For the best Tokyo skyline views, book 45 to 60 minutes before sunset. You need time for security, lockers, elevators, and finding a good viewing spot.

- Want the iconic Mount Fuji view from Shibuya Sky? Winter and early spring usually offer the clearest visibility. Sunset combined with a clear Fuji day is basically the jackpot of Tokyo photography.

- Once you're inside, there is no general time limit. Arrive early and stay through daylight, golden hour, blue hour, and Tokyo's night lights.

- If sunset tickets are gone, don't panic. A slot one hour earlier gives almost the exact same Shibuya Sky experience and often has much better availability.

- Rainy forecast? Still go. Some of the most dramatic Tokyo views happen after rain, when the city lights reflect across Shibuya Crossing and the skyline glows beneath low clouds.

For most first time Japan travelers, Shibuya Sky, TeamLab Planets, and Tokyo DisneySea are the three attractions that sell out fastest. Plan them before booking restaurants, shopping, or anything else.

Tucked away next to the famous Arashiyama bamboo forest in Kyoto is the Mikami Shrine, the only shrine dedicated to hair. 

That’s because the shrine is dedicated to Fujiwara Unemenosuke Masayuki who is said to have been the first hairdresser in Japan. 

He was so revered for his work that for hundreds of years barbers and hair salons closed their shops on the day of his death each month, the 17th. 

These days many barbers and stylists visit his shrine to ask for blessings, or those who are taking the national barber and beautician exam. 

In addition, those who have wishes regarding hair visit the shrine—which includes me. 

After years of hair loss, I figured why not give it a try. 

The way you pray at this shrine is unique. You buy a prayer envelope and then the priests cuts a small strand of your hair and puts it in the envelope. 

You pray at the Kami (Masayuki Fujiwara) before giving the envelope back to the priest who will pray for the wellbeing of your hair. 

I don’t really believe in this stuff but it’s worth a shot.

Why is it that suddenly in the past 2 years so many Indians have been going to Japan?

What did the Japanese government do to create this hype that back in 2019, the number of Indians traveling to Japan were 175k...

By 2024, that number jumped by 40% to 233k...

And by 2025, it jumped further by 35% growth in a year to 315k...

Now there are three broad reasons for this...

👉🏻 1/ Back in 2019, the Japanese Yen was worth 66 Paise... which fell by almost 20% in 2024 to 54 paise...

So something that cost you Rs 1000 to buy in Japan in 2019 became Rs 800 by 2024... everything from hotels, to food, to trains was cheaper by 20%

And Japan was falling faster than the Rupee compared to the USD and EUR and GBP because Japan was lending money at almost 0%, which people would borrow and put into the US markets and bonds to earn over 5%...

This Yen Carry Trade was leading to depletion in the value of the Yen... because all that borrowed Yen would have to be sold in the forex market to convert to Dollars. That selling kept eroding the value.

Even now in 2026, when the Yen is back to its previous exchange rate of 60 paise, it’s still 10% cheaper than 2019... and by comparison all other currencies are up

👉🏻 2/ Air India and Japan Airlines started direct routes in 2024 from Delhi, which further brought down the cost of travel as there would not be as many interconnecting flights in between

👉🏻 3/ Non EU passport holders as visitors, which includes Indians, get a 10% discount on consumption tax by buying things in Japan - so shopping is cheaper compared to other countries where tax refund is lower than 10%... and you could get this tax refund instantly at checkout rather than claiming it at the airport

👉🏻 4/ And then of course, the visa process became electronic from 2024, which made it easier to apply and get

3 things you should NEVER bring to Japan 🇯🇵 
Save this before you pack!

1. YOUR HAIR DRYER, STRAIGHTENER, CURLING IRON

The thing nobody warns you about: Japan runs on 100 volts, the lowest of any developed country in the world.

🌍 Voltage reality:
🇺🇸 USA (120V): works, but underpowered 
🇪🇺 Europe (220-240V): will NOT work without a heavy transformer
🇬🇧 UK (240V): will NOT work without a transformer
🇦🇺 Australia (240V): will NOT work without a transformer
🇯🇵 Dual-voltage tools (100-240V): ✅ work fine

What to do instead:
✅ Use the hotel hair dryer
✅ Buy a dual-voltage travel dryer
✅ Buy a cheap Japanese one at Don Quijote, BIC Camera, or Yodobashi (¥2,000-5,000)

2. YOUR USUAL MEDICATIONS

Japan bans an unexpected list of common Western medications. Tourists get stopped at customs regularly for bringing what they consider routine pills

- Cold medicines containing pseudoephedrine
- Many ADHD medications and stimulants
- Strong opioid painkillers
- Certain allergy medications
- Some inhalers

 3. TOO MUCH STUFF

Travel to Japan with a half-empty suitcase. 
Even if you’re not a shopaholic, Japan retail is dangerously good:

🛍️ Uniqlo, MUJI, Don Quijote- Japanese prices are home prices
🛍️ Konbini snacks - KitKats, Pocky, sembei
🛍️ Beauty products - sunscreens, skincare, 
makeup
🛍️ Stationery 
🛍️ Kitchen tools 
🛍️ Furikake, sake, tea, hand-painted ceramics
🛍️ Anime, manga, gaming merch

BONUS TIP — BUY A NEW SUITCASE IN JAPAN

🛒 Don Quijote sells brand-name luggage at 
radical discounts:
- Samsonite 50-70% off other countries
- American Tourister, Travelpro also deeply discounted
- Plus tax-free shopping knocks off another 10%

The smartest souvenir you’ll bring home from Japan is the suitcase carrying all your other souvenirs.

🚫 Don't Visit Japan During These Times!
Thinking of traveling to Japan? 🇯🇵 Better check your calendar first! While Japan is beautiful year-round, these 4 seasons/periods might just make you regret your trip:
1️⃣ Golden Week (Late April - Early May): The entire country is on the move! Crowded trains, expensive hotels, and long lines everywhere. 😱
2️⃣ New Year’s (Late Dec - Early Jan): It's family time in Japan, which means many shops and restaurants are closed. You might find yourself in a very quiet city! 🎍
3️⃣ August: Think you can handle the heat? Think again! The humidity in Japan is on another level. You’ll feel like you’re melting! 🥵💦
4️⃣ Pollen Season (Feb - April): If you have hay fever, be prepared! Even if you don't, you might develop it here. Don't forget your masks and tissues! 🤧

Collecting eki stamps in Japan! 💮

If you’re new to eki stamps, here’s how it works: 

Look for stamps at train stations, museums, temples, castles, and popular tourist spots! Each stamp is one-of-a-kind, so it’s a unique (and free!) way to document your trip in Japan! 

If you’re having trouble finding a stamp, just ask train station employees, “スタンプありますか?” (“Stampu arimasu ka?”) or “駅スタンプはどこですか?” (“Eki stamp wa doko desu ka?”), and they’ll help you out.

Pro tips:
💮Bring your own stamp pad: Sometimes the ink pads at stations can be dry, so having your own guarantees a clear stamp every time!

📚Get a small notebook: Many travelers bring a dedicated notebook to keep all their stamps together (like me!), creating a fun travel journal by the end of their trip.

🔖Look for limited edition stamps: Some locations offer special stamps for seasonal events or anniversaries, so keep an eye out for these rare ones!

Happy collecting, and enjoy making your own personalized travel souvenir! 🗾

If you’re a cat lover in Tokyo, Myogi Shrine belongs at the top of your itinerary. Located in Komagome, just a short walk from the station, this peaceful sanctuary is a must-visit for anyone who finds magic in feline folklore. 🐾

The Legend of the Lucky Guide ⛩️
The shrine’s feline connection dates back to the 15th century. Legend has it that the samurai Ota Dokan was guided to safety during a dark battle by a mysterious black cat. In honor of this “lucky guide,” the shrine features rare Komainekko (guardian cats) instead of the traditional lion-dogs. Today, it’s a beautiful place to pray for the health and happiness of our pets.

Stained-Glass Treasures 😍
The omamori (amulets) here are truly works of art. Known for their “stained-glass” style, these translucent acrylic charms glow vibrantly when they catch the light. At 2,000 yen, they are some of the most unique keepsakes you’ll find in Japan.

Meet Mugi-kun 🐱
If you’re lucky, you’ll meet the resident shrine cat, Mugi-kun. He’s a fluffy, 7-year-old male who usually greets visitors near the reception. Even on a rainy day, his presence makes the grounds feel incredibly serene.

This is Yufuin, a small town in Kyushu, and one of the most charming onsen towns in Japan

📍 WHERE IT IS
Yufuin is in southern Japan (Kyushu), about 2 hours from Fukuoka

✈️ HOW TO GET HERE
Option 1: Fly into Fukuoka Airport
→ Train (Yufuin no Mori or local train) 2–2.5 hours

Option 2: From Osaka
→ Fly to Fukuoka (1 hour) + train 
→ Or Shinkansen to Hakata + transfer (3–4 hours total)

♨️ WHAT TO DO
* Walk Yunotsubo Street (shops, food, souvenirs)
* Visit Lake Kinrin (best early morning)
* Stay in a ryokan with a private onsen
* Try local desserts + street food

📍 WHAT’S NEARBY
* Beppu → 30 mins (famous for steaming hot springs + “Hells”)
* Mount Aso → 1.5–2 hours (volcano + open landscapes)
* Kurokawa Onsen → 1 hour (more traditional, quiet onsen town)

🚗 HOW TO GET AROUND
* Trains between major towns

This shrine, called the Motonosumi Shrine is in the Yamaguchi prefecture. It was built in 1955 by a man who claimed to have seen a  white fox by his bedside, after which he built a shrine to appease the gods. 

It sits along the coast of the Sea of Japan. 

The guide we visited with told us to make a wish and if the wish comes true, we must return and offer our gratitude - that is the belief there. 

We booked a day trip from Fukuoka to reach here. (Booked on klook)

And like I said before , Kyushu trip took my breath away at every single stop… and this shrine felt like something out of a dream.
